key_insight: 3M Command strips work by using a highly extensible adhesive that carries load while attached, then greatly lowers release force as it stretches and progressively debonds under peeling.
engineering_relevance: The paper gives a fracture-mechanics design rule showing that load capacity can far exceed removal force when bonded length is large relative to adhesive thickness, helping optimize removable adhesives for strong hold and clean release.
method: Derived analytical energy-release-rate expressions for interfacial cracks under loading and release.
Validated the theory with finite-element J-integral calculations.
Mapped alternating crack paths and failure envelopes for competing interfacial toughnesses.
evidence_signal: The analytical results were checked against finite-element J-integral evaluations, and parametric studies were used to generate predictive failure envelopes.
watch_item: The linear scaling and crack-path predictions depend on idealized geometry and material assumptions, so real products may deviate due to viscoelasticity, rate effects, or surface variability.
paper_title: How do 3M Command strips work? A fracture mechanics approach
